title: Wet-felted needle book
subtitle: A folded felt case for hand-sewing needles, 10 × 8 cm, in merino and wool pages
excerpt: A compact needle book made from two pieces of fully fulled merino felt: a firm outer cover and softer contrasting-colour inner pages. Folded and stitched at the spine, finished size 10 × 8 cm closed. The felt must be fully fulled so needles do not pierce through the pages when stored.
sourceNotes: Wet-felted needle books are a standard beginner project in UK needlework and fibre arts circles. Technique synthesised from general flat wet-felting and hand-sewing practice; not drawn from a specific PD source.

glossaryTerms.definition: Combed and drawn wool fibres in a continuous rope, ready for wet felting or spinning.
The final agitation stage of wet felting in which fibres are compressed and shrunk by rubbing, rolling, and throwing, producing a dense, firm fabric.
In bookbinding and needle books, the spine is the fold or seam that joins the front and back covers and the inner pages.
A laying technique in which successive tufts of roving are placed at 90 degrees to the previous layer, producing even felting in all directions.

content.text: A needle book keeps hand-sewing needles organised and prevents them from getting lost or rusting. This one is made from two flat panels of fully
fulled
merino felt: a firm outer cover and two or three softer inner pages in a contrasting colour. The cover and pages are cut to size, layered, and stitched together along the
spine

. The closed size is 10 × 8 cm. Dense fulling is essential: needles stored in loosely felted pages will work through the fibres and escape. This project involves making two small flat felt panels from scratch, then sewing them together.

items.name: Merino roving, 19-21 micron, for the outer cover
Merino roving or fine wool roving in a contrasting colour, for the inner pages
A blunt darning needle and wool thread for stitching the spine

content.text: Method
Step 1: Make the outer cover panel
Lay
roving
on the felting mat in
cross-hatch layers

: four layers total in a rectangle approximately 24 × 18 cm (the panel will shrink to 20 × 16 cm after fulling, which you then cut to 20 × 8 cm for the folded cover). Wet down with warm soapy water, press flat, and rub for 10-12 minutes until a firm pre-felt forms. Roll in the bamboo mat for 20 minutes, turning 90 degrees every 5 minutes. Full by scrunching and throwing until the panel is firm and at least 3-4 mm thick. Rinse and let dry.
Step 2: Make the inner page panels

Repeat the same process with the contrasting roving: four cross-hatch layers in a 22 × 16 cm rectangle, fulled until dense. The inner pages can be slightly thinner (2-3 mm) than the outer cover. Rinse and let dry.
Step 3: Cut to size

Once both panels are fully dry, cut each to the following dimensions: outer cover panel to 20 × 8 cm; inner page panels to 9.5 × 7.5 cm (cut two or three identical pages). Fully fulled felt cuts cleanly with sharp scissors and does not fray. Take time to cut straight edges: a ruler and chalk marker help here.
Step 4: Assemble the needle book

Fold the outer cover panel in half to form a 10 × 8 cm booklet shape. Stack the inner page panels on top of each other and lay them inside the folded cover, centred left-to-right and top-to-bottom. The pages should sit about 2-3 mm inside the cover edge on all sides.
Step 5: Stitch the spine

Thread a blunt darning needle with a length of wool thread in a complementary colour. Work a running stitch or blanket stitch along the fold (the spine) to secure the pages inside the cover. Stitch through all layers. Knot the thread firmly at each end. The stitching along the spine is the only thing holding the pages in place, so make it neat and secure.

items.problem: Needles push through the inner pages and stick out the other side
items.cause: The inner page panels were not fulled densely enough
items.fix: Return the inner page panel to warm soapy water and full more aggressively before cutting. The felt needs to be firm enough that a needle pressed against the surface meets firm resistance.

items.problem: The cover panel has a thick, springy feel but is not flat
items.cause: The roving layers were uneven, producing a bumpy surface
items.fix: Full the panel under a flat weight (a heavy book on top of a damp cloth) while it is still wet to press it flat as it dries.

items.cause: Scissors were not sharp enough, or the felt was not fully dry when cut
items.fix: Let the felt dry completely before cutting. Use sharp fabric scissors, not general craft scissors. Cut in a single smooth stroke rather than short snips.

content.text: Care and finishing

The needle book does not need washing in normal use. If it becomes dusty, spot-clean with a barely damp cloth. Store flat to maintain the cover shape. The needle book can be personalised with small embroidered initials or a needle-felted motif on the front cover before the pages are inserted.
